meta.title: Drop Score — Regenerative Factor-Model Knowledge Graph
meta.purpose: The sub-graph behind the 'Drop Score' node of the Farmer Drop knowledge map: a guidance tree that defines, in fine-grained evaluable terms, what regenerative operating practices actually mean for a small producer.
meta.no_weights_note: This is a knowledge graph, not a scoring formula. There are deliberately NO weights and NO scores. Each node defines a concept and states what evidence would demonstrate it. A Drop Score is the transparent set of indicators a producer can evidence, never a single opaque number.
meta.structure: 4 tiers: (0) root, (1) domains, (2) factors, (3) fine-grained evaluable indicators. Edges: 'comprises' (root->domain, domain->factor), 'evidenced_by' (factor->indicator), and cross-domain 'related' links.

sources.name: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators
Noble Research Institute — 6 Soil Health Principles

nodes.label: Drop Score
nodes.plain_meaning: A guidance tree that spells out what regenerative food production really means, in terms a small farmer can see and evidence — not one black-box number.
nodes.why_it_matters: It turns a fuzzy marketing word ('regenerative') into concrete, checkable practices, so buyers can trust it and farmers know exactly what to improve next.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Coverage and evidence across the leaf indicators below — how much of the tree a producer can actually demonstrate.
nodes.data_source: A composite of soil tests, satellite imagery, photo evidence, receipts, self-report + spot-audit, and third-party certifications across every branch.
nodes.gaming_resistance: No hidden weights to reverse-engineer: the tree only exposes transparent evidence on named indicators, and cross-checks self-reports against physical soil and satellite data that are hard to fake.

nodes.label: Regenerative — Soil
nodes.plain_meaning: The living-soil foundation: are you building soil organic matter, keeping the ground covered and rooted, and disturbing it as little as possible?
nodes.why_it_matters: Soil health is the root system of everything regenerative — carbon, water, nutrients and resilience all flow from it (the four NRCS principles).
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Evidence across the five soil factors below: cover, disturbance, living roots, carbon, and water/structure.
nodes.data_source: Accredited soil tests, in-field infiltration tests, dated photos, and satellite NDVI.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Physical soil measurements (organic matter, aggregate stability, infiltration) change slowly and are lab-verifiable — you cannot fake a multi-year soil-test trend.
refs: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators

nodes.label: Diversity
nodes.plain_meaning: How many genuinely different crops, animals, varieties, habitats and income streams your farm runs — the opposite of a single-commodity monoculture.
nodes.why_it_matters: Diversity is the founder's core factor family; it drives soil health, pest resilience, income stability and biodiversity all at once.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Counts and evenness across crops/rotation, genetics, enterprises, non-crop habitat, and market channels.
nodes.data_source: Planting and sales records, seed/breed receipts, and aerial/satellite imagery.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Diversity claims are cross-checked against sales ledgers and imagery — you cannot sell or show what you did not actually grow.

nodes.label: Operating Practices
nodes.plain_meaning: The day-to-day management choices: how you handle inputs, nutrients, animals, water, waste and energy.
nodes.why_it_matters: Practices are where regeneration is actually done; ROC and Regenified verify practices alongside measured outcomes.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Evidence across input reduction, nutrient management, grazing integration, water stewardship, and waste/energy.
nodes.data_source: Purchase receipts, application and spray records, meter readings, and certifications.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Input and resource use leave a paper trail (purchases, meters, logs) that is hard to understate without falsifying records.

nodes.label: Animal & Livestock Welfare
nodes.plain_meaning: Whether animals on the farm live well — out on pasture, handled gently, healthy, and free from routine mutilations.
nodes.why_it_matters: ROC makes animal welfare a required pillar; for livestock farms it is inseparable from regenerative land use.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Pasture access, space, low-stress handling and herd/flock health.
nodes.data_source: Site audit, dated photos, veterinary records, and third-party certification.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Welfare shows in the animals and facilities on an unannounced site visit and in vet records — it is hard to stage herd-wide.

nodes.label: Community & Local Economy
nodes.plain_meaning: How the farm treats its people and its place — fair pay and safe work, and food that actually reaches the local community.
nodes.why_it_matters: ROC's third pillar is social fairness; Farmer Drop's whole thesis is returning value to farmers and local eaters.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Fair-labor evidence and local sourcing/access across the enterprise.
nodes.data_source: Payroll records, confidential worker interviews, sales-geography data, third-party certification.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Wage and sourcing claims are checkable against payroll and delivery records and via worker interviews — far harder to fake than a self-declaration.

nodes.label: Soil Organic Matter & Carbon
nodes.plain_meaning: The amount of living and once-living carbon in your soil — the dark, spongy stuff that feeds microbes and holds water.
nodes.why_it_matters: Organic matter is the single best summary of soil function: more carbon means more fertility, water storage and resilience, and it underpins carbon markets.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Soil organic matter % and its multi-year trend, plus lab carbon-mineralization (respiration).
nodes.data_source: Accredited soil-lab test (soil test).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Requires a dated lab test from georeferenced sample points; trends need repeated sampling, so one good number cannot stand in for real improvement.
refs: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators

nodes.label: Soil organic matter % trend
nodes.plain_meaning: Your measured soil organic matter percentage, tracked the same way in the same spots year after year.
nodes.why_it_matters: A rising SOM trend is the clearest proof soil is actually regenerating, not just being maintained.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: SOM % from a standard soil test, compared to a baseline from the same GPS-tagged points.
nodes.data_source: Soil test (accredited lab).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Fixed, georeferenced sample locations and lab chain-of-custody stop cherry-picking the best patch of the field.
refs: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators

nodes.label: Soil biological activity (respiration)
nodes.plain_meaning: How much your soil 'breathes' — a quick lab test of how alive the soil microbes are.
nodes.why_it_matters: Living soil cycles nutrients for free; respiration responds faster than carbon, so it is an early sign practices are working.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: CO2 burst / carbon-mineralization from a rewetted soil sample (e.g. 24-hour respiration).
nodes.data_source: Soil test (lab).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Run by a lab on a submitted sample; hard to spoof without genuinely biologically active soil.
refs: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators

nodes.label: Soil Cover / Armor
nodes.plain_meaning: Keeping the soil surface covered with living plants or crop residue instead of leaving bare dirt.
nodes.why_it_matters: Bare soil bakes, erodes and loses carbon; NRCS calls cover 'soil armor', the first principle of soil health.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Percentage of the year and of the field that stays covered by residue or living plants.
nodes.data_source: Dated field photos + satellite NDVI (photo evidence / satellite).
nodes.gaming_resistance: A satellite time-series shows cover across the whole field on real dates, so a single staged photo of a covered strip will not hold up.

nodes.label: Year-round ground cover %
nodes.plain_meaning: How much of your ground stays covered by living plants or mulch/residue through the whole year, including winter.
nodes.why_it_matters: Continuous cover protects soil, feeds microbes and stops erosion between cash crops.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Estimated % ground cover across the year (ROC-style bands: 25-50 / 50-75 / 75-100%).
nodes.data_source: Satellite NDVI + dated photos; ROC audit for certified farms.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Whole-field satellite coverage over the calendar year is hard to fake and is audited under ROC.

nodes.label: Bare-soil / erosion check
nodes.plain_meaning: Spotting and reducing patches of bare, exposed or eroding soil.
nodes.why_it_matters: Bare soil is where carbon and topsoil are lost first; EOV uses bare-ground as a core leading indicator.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: % bare ground along a fixed transect / photo points, plus visible erosion (rills, gullies).
nodes.data_source: Photo evidence + self-report with spot-audit; EOV short-term monitoring.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Transect-based, geolocated photo points sampled by a monitor make selective framing hard.

nodes.label: Tillage & Disturbance
nodes.plain_meaning: How much you physically disturb the soil by plowing, digging or discing.
nodes.why_it_matters: Tillage breaks soil structure and burns organic matter; minimizing disturbance is NRCS principle two.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Number and depth of tillage passes per year, and the share of acres in no-till / reduced-till.
nodes.data_source: Equipment/operation logs + receipts, with self-report and spot-audit.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Tillage leaves visible signatures (residue burial, broken structure) that a monitor or satellite can corroborate against the claim.

nodes.label: Tillage intensity (passes/year)
nodes.plain_meaning: How many times, and how deep, you turn the soil each season.
nodes.why_it_matters: Fewer, shallower passes mean less carbon lost and less structural damage.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Count of tillage operations per field per year and their depth.
nodes.data_source: Operation log / equipment receipts (self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Cross-checked against fuel/equipment use and the visible degree of residue incorporation in the field.

nodes.label: No-till / direct seeding
nodes.plain_meaning: Planting straight into last year's residue without plowing first.
nodes.why_it_matters: No-till is the gold-standard low-disturbance practice (ROC Gold requires it).
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Share of planted acres established with no-till / direct-seed equipment.
nodes.data_source: Planting records + photo of residue at planting; ROC audit.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Residue left standing at planting is physically visible on the surface and in imagery.

nodes.label: Living Roots
nodes.plain_meaning: Keeping something growing with live roots in the ground for as much of the year as possible.
nodes.why_it_matters: Living roots feed soil microbes year-round (NRCS principle four) and are what actually build soil carbon.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Number of days per year the field has a living root, and whether cover crops fill the gaps between cash crops.
nodes.data_source: Cropping calendar + satellite NDVI green-up.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Satellite greenness confirms when the field was actually growing, not just the planting intention.

nodes.label: Cover cropping
nodes.plain_meaning: Growing a non-cash crop (rye, clover, or a diverse mix) to protect and feed the soil between main crops.
nodes.why_it_matters: Cover crops deliver cover, living roots and diversity all at once — the workhorse regenerative practice.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Acres and species of cover crop planted, and whether it is a single species or a diverse mix.
nodes.data_source: Seed receipts + dated photos + NDVI (receipts / satellite).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Seed-purchase receipts plus satellite green-up in the off-season are hard to fabricate together.

nodes.label: Continuous living-root days
nodes.plain_meaning: The number of days each year your ground has a living, growing root in it.
nodes.why_it_matters: More living-root days is the most direct driver of soil carbon gain.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Days/year with green, growing cover, derived from the cropping calendar and NDVI.
nodes.data_source: Satellite NDVI time-series + cropping calendar.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Derived from independent satellite greenness, not just the farmer's word.

nodes.label: Water Infiltration & Structure
nodes.plain_meaning: How quickly rain soaks into your soil instead of running off — a sign of good structure.
nodes.why_it_matters: Well-structured soil captures water, resists drought and flooding, and holds nutrients (an EOV lagging indicator).
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Infiltration rate (minutes for an inch of water) and soil aggregate stability.
nodes.data_source: In-field infiltration ring test + lab aggregate-stability test.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Ring-infiltration and slake tests are done on-site or in a lab on real soil and cannot be talked up.
refs: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators

nodes.label: Water infiltration rate
nodes.plain_meaning: A simple ring-and-stopwatch test of how fast an inch of water sinks into your soil.
nodes.why_it_matters: Faster infiltration means more rain captured and less erosion — you feel it in a dry year.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Time for a fixed volume of water to infiltrate, repeated at set points.
nodes.data_source: In-field infiltration ring test (self-report + spot-audit); EOV long-term monitoring.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Standardized method at georeferenced points, ideally witnessed by a monitor.

nodes.label: Soil aggregate stability
nodes.plain_meaning: How well your soil crumbs hold together when wet instead of turning to mud (the 'slake test').
nodes.why_it_matters: Stable aggregates mean good structure, aeration and erosion resistance — a core SHI indicator.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Aggregate-stability score from a lab or a standardized slake test.
nodes.data_source: Soil test (lab) or witnessed slake test.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Lab-measured on submitted soil; reflects real long-term biology, not a quick fix.
refs: Soil Health Institute — Minimum Suite of Soil Health Indicators

nodes.label: Crop & Rotational Diversity
nodes.plain_meaning: Growing many different crops and rotating them through your fields instead of the same one every year.
nodes.why_it_matters: Rotational diversity breaks pest cycles, spreads risk and feeds soil biology (NRCS principle three; ROC counts crops in rotation).
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Number of distinct crops grown and number of crops in each field's rotation.
nodes.data_source: Planting/harvest records + aerial imagery.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Crop identity is visible in imagery and provable by harvest and sales records.

nodes.label: Crops in rotation
nodes.plain_meaning: How many different crops you cycle through the same ground over a few years.
nodes.why_it_matters: Longer rotations (ROC Gold wants 7+) mean healthier soil and fewer pests; a 5-crop Alberta rotation already scores well.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Count of distinct crops in the field's multi-year rotation.
nodes.data_source: Field-history records; ROC audit.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Multi-year field histories are cross-checked against imagery of what actually grew.

nodes.label: Intercropping / polyculture
nodes.plain_meaning: Growing more than one crop in the same field at once — a mixed market-garden bed, companion planting, or alley cropping.
nodes.why_it_matters: Mixing species in space, not just time, boosts diversity and yield stability on small plots.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Presence and area of intercropped or multi-species plantings.
nodes.data_source: Dated photos + planting map (photo evidence).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Visible in the field and in close photos; small-plot layouts are auditable in a single visit.

nodes.label: Genetic & Varietal Diversity
nodes.plain_meaning: How many different varieties and breeds you keep — including old, rare and locally-adapted ones.
nodes.why_it_matters: Genetic diversity protects against disease wipe-outs and conserves heritage genetics (FAO tracks genetic erosion).
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Number of crop varieties and livestock breeds, plus presence of heirloom / rare genetics.
nodes.data_source: Seed/breed receipts + a variety list.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Variety and breed claims tie to seed/stock purchase records and are visually distinct on a visit.

nodes.label: Varieties & breeds kept
nodes.plain_meaning: The number of distinct crop varieties and animal breeds on your farm.
nodes.why_it_matters: More genetic options mean more resilience to new pests, disease and climate swings.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Count of named varieties/breeds actively grown or raised.
nodes.data_source: Seed/stock receipts + self-report with spot-audit.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Backed by purchase records; an on-farm spot-check confirms they are actually present.

nodes.label: Heirloom / rare & adapted genetics
nodes.plain_meaning: Growing open-pollinated or heirloom seed and keeping rare or heritage animal breeds, and saving your own seed.
nodes.why_it_matters: These genetics are being lost globally; keeping them is real conservation and often better local performance.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Presence of open-pollinated/heirloom lines or recognized rare breeds, and seed-saving.
nodes.data_source: Seed-source records, breed registries (third-party cert / receipts).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Rare-breed registries and heirloom seed sources are documented and independently verifiable.

nodes.label: Enterprise & Income Diversity
nodes.plain_meaning: How many different products or income streams your farm sells, so you are not betting everything on one commodity.
nodes.why_it_matters: Enterprise diversity is what frees a farmer from commodity lock-in — the whole point of Farmer Drop for the producer.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Number of distinct revenue-generating enterprises and how concentrated income is in the top one.
nodes.data_source: Sales / accounting records (receipts / self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Income mix is provable from real sales ledgers, not from claims.

nodes.label: Distinct enterprises
nodes.plain_meaning: How many separate things you sell — vegetables, beef, eggs, honey, value-added goods, agritourism, and so on.
nodes.why_it_matters: Each independent enterprise is a buffer when one crop or market fails.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Count of enterprises contributing a meaningful share of revenue.
nodes.data_source: Accounting records (self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Tied to actual sales lines; a token enterprise with no sales does not count.

nodes.label: Income concentration
nodes.plain_meaning: What share of your money comes from your single biggest product.
nodes.why_it_matters: A farm earning 90% from one crop is fragile; lower concentration means more resilience.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Revenue share of the top enterprise (lower is more diverse).
nodes.data_source: Accounting / sales records.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Computed from the full sales ledger, so hiding concentration means falsifying the accounts.

nodes.label: Habitat & Non-crop Biodiversity
nodes.plain_meaning: The wild edges of your farm — hedgerows, buffer strips, ponds, trees and flowering habitat that are not crops.
nodes.why_it_matters: Non-crop habitat hosts pollinators and pest predators and is a headline agrobiodiversity indicator (landscape complexity).
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Area and connectivity of non-crop habitat, plus presence of pollinator/beneficial habitat.
nodes.data_source: Aerial / satellite imagery + field map.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Habitat area and hedgerows are directly visible from the air and persistent over time.

nodes.label: Non-crop habitat & buffers
nodes.plain_meaning: Set-aside strips, hedgerows, woodlots, wetlands and field margins left for nature.
nodes.why_it_matters: These refuges drive on-farm biodiversity and natural pest control, reducing the need to spray.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: % of farm area and length of edges in permanent non-crop habitat.
nodes.data_source: Satellite / aerial imagery + farm map.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Persistent habitat features are unmistakable in multi-date imagery.

nodes.label: Pollinator & beneficial habitat
nodes.plain_meaning: Flowering strips, insectaries and undisturbed nesting areas that support bees and beneficial insects.
nodes.why_it_matters: Pollinators and predator insects are free labor; supporting them lifts yield and cuts pesticide use.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Presence and area of flowering/beneficial habitat and reduced-mowing zones.
nodes.data_source: Dated photos in bloom + map (photo evidence).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Seasonal bloom photos plus mapped locations are checkable on a site visit.

nodes.label: Market-channel Diversity
nodes.plain_meaning: How many different ways you sell — farmers markets, CSA, direct-to-eater, restaurants, wholesale, and Farmer Drop itself.
nodes.why_it_matters: Multiple channels protect a farm if one buyer walks, and direct channels capture the margin Farmer Drop is built to return.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Number of active sales channels and the share sold direct-to-eater.
nodes.data_source: Sales records by channel (receipts / self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Channel mix is provable from invoices and platform records.

nodes.label: Active market channels
nodes.plain_meaning: The number of distinct places or ways you actually sell your product.
nodes.why_it_matters: More channels means less dependence on any single buyer or price.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Count of channels with real sales in the last year.
nodes.data_source: Sales records by channel.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Each channel must show real transactions to count.

nodes.label: Direct-to-eater share
nodes.plain_meaning: How much of what you sell goes straight to the person who eats it, versus through middlemen.
nodes.why_it_matters: Direct sales are where farmers keep the most margin — the core wedge of the Farmer Drop model.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: % of revenue sold direct (market, CSA, on-platform).
nodes.data_source: Sales records; Farmer Drop platform data.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Platform and invoice records make the direct share directly measurable.

nodes.label: Input Use & Chemical Reduction
nodes.plain_meaning: Cutting back on synthetic pesticides, herbicides and fungicides, and the chemicals that harm soil life.
nodes.why_it_matters: Reducing biocides is central to regenerative and organic standards; it protects soil biology and beneficial insects.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Quantity of synthetic pesticide/herbicide applied per acre, and use of IPM instead of routine spraying.
nodes.data_source: Input-purchase receipts + spray records (receipts).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Purchase receipts and mandated spray logs bound how much could have been applied.

nodes.label: Synthetic pesticide/herbicide reduction
nodes.plain_meaning: How little synthetic pesticide and herbicide you use per acre, ideally trending toward none.
nodes.why_it_matters: These chemicals are the biggest disruptor of soil and insect life; less is directly regenerative.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Active-ingredient quantity per acre per year, and % of acres treated.
nodes.data_source: Purchase receipts + spray logs (receipts / self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Bounded by purchase records and regulator-required application logs; over-claiming means underreporting purchases.

nodes.label: Integrated pest management
nodes.plain_meaning: Managing pests with scouting, thresholds, beneficial insects and rotation before reaching for a spray.
nodes.why_it_matters: IPM proves reductions come from real management, not luck, and it is durable.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Documented scouting/threshold records and use of biological and cultural controls.
nodes.data_source: Scouting logs + advisor records (self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Contemporaneous scouting logs and advisor sign-off are harder to backfill than a simple claim.

nodes.label: Nutrient Management
nodes.plain_meaning: Feeding your crops with compost, manure and precise, soil-test-based fertility instead of blanket synthetic fertilizer.
nodes.why_it_matters: Good nutrient management builds soil, cuts runoff and pollution, and saves money.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Use of organic amendments, a nutrient budget matched to soil tests, and reduced synthetic nitrogen.
nodes.data_source: Amendment receipts + soil-test-based nutrient plan (receipts / soil test).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Compost/manure volumes and a soil-test-linked plan are documented and physically visible.

nodes.label: Compost & organic amendments
nodes.plain_meaning: Adding compost, well-managed manure or other organic matter to feed the soil.
nodes.why_it_matters: Organic amendments build the carbon and biology that synthetic fertilizer alone cannot.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Tons/acre of compost or manure applied, and whether composting happens on-farm.
nodes.data_source: Amendment receipts / compost logs + photos.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Volumes tie to receipts or to an on-site compost operation you can inspect.

nodes.label: Soil-test-based fertility
nodes.plain_meaning: Applying nutrients to a plan built from soil tests, so you add what the crop needs and no more.
nodes.why_it_matters: Right-rate, right-time fertility prevents the over-application that pollutes water and wastes money.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: A current nutrient budget tied to recent soil tests, and synthetic-N rate versus crop need.
nodes.data_source: Soil test + written nutrient plan (soil test / self-report).
nodes.gaming_resistance: The plan must reference dated lab soil tests, anchoring claims to real data.

nodes.label: Grazing & Livestock Integration
nodes.plain_meaning: Using animals well — moving them across pasture and integrating them with crops so they build soil instead of degrading it.
nodes.why_it_matters: Adaptive grazing and crop-livestock integration are central to Savory/Regenified and turn animals into a soil-building tool.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Rotational/adaptive grazing in place, and livestock integrated onto cropland (grazing residues / cover crops).
nodes.data_source: Grazing charts + photos (self-report + spot-audit); EOV.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Pasture recovery and even utilization are visible in the field and in imagery over a season.

nodes.label: Rotational / adaptive grazing
nodes.plain_meaning: Moving livestock through paddocks and giving grass time to fully recover before grazing it again.
nodes.why_it_matters: Planned recovery grows more grass, more roots and more soil carbon than continuous grazing — the Savory core.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Evidence of paddock moves, rest periods, and the residual cover left after grazing.
nodes.data_source: Grazing chart / move records + pasture photos (self-report + spot-audit); EOV monitoring.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Rested paddocks and left residual are physically visible; EOV transects verify pasture condition independently.
refs: Noble Research Institute — 6 Soil Health Principles

nodes.label: Crop-livestock integration
nodes.plain_meaning: Grazing animals on cover crops or crop residues, or rotating pasture and cropland.
nodes.why_it_matters: Integration recycles nutrients on-farm, adds diversity and living roots, and cuts input costs.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Acres where livestock graze cover crops/residues or rotate with cropping.
nodes.data_source: Field records + photos (self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Animal impact (dung, grazed residue) is visible on the specific fields claimed.

nodes.label: Water Stewardship
nodes.plain_meaning: Using irrigation water efficiently and protecting streams, ponds and wetlands on and near your land.
nodes.why_it_matters: Water is the limiting resource on many farms; efficiency and waterway protection safeguard the whole watershed.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Irrigation efficiency (water applied vs crop need) and protected/buffered waterways.
nodes.data_source: Water-meter readings + aerial imagery of buffers.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Metered water use and visible riparian buffers are both independently checkable.

nodes.label: Irrigation efficiency
nodes.plain_meaning: Getting more crop per drop — using drip, scheduling and soil-moisture data instead of over-watering.
nodes.why_it_matters: Efficient irrigation saves water and money and prevents nutrient leaching.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Water applied per acre versus crop requirement, and efficient-irrigation tech in use.
nodes.data_source: Water-meter / utility readings (self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Anchored to metered volumes, which are hard to overstate downward.

nodes.label: Waterway protection & buffers
nodes.plain_meaning: Keeping vegetated buffer strips and fencing livestock out of streams, ponds and wetlands.
nodes.why_it_matters: Buffers filter runoff, stop erosion and protect the water everyone downstream drinks.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Presence and width of vegetated buffers along waterways, and livestock exclusion.
nodes.data_source: Aerial imagery + field map (satellite / photo evidence).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Buffer strips and stream fencing are clearly visible in imagery and on the ground.

nodes.label: Waste & Energy
nodes.plain_meaning: Recycling on-farm waste back into the land and using energy efficiently or from renewable sources.
nodes.why_it_matters: Closing waste loops and cutting fossil energy shrink the farm's footprint and often its costs.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: On-farm organic recycling/composting, plus energy source and efficiency measures.
nodes.data_source: Utility bills + photos of composting/renewables (receipts / photo evidence).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Energy bills and visible composting or solar installations are concrete evidence.

nodes.label: On-farm waste recycling
nodes.plain_meaning: Turning crop residues, manure and food waste into compost or feed instead of dumping or burning them.
nodes.why_it_matters: Recycling nutrients on-farm builds soil and removes disposal cost and pollution.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Share of organic waste composted / reused on-farm, and no residue burning.
nodes.data_source: Photos + compost logs (photo evidence / self-report).
nodes.gaming_resistance: An on-site composting operation is inspectable; burning leaves visible scorch and satellite heat signatures.

nodes.label: Energy source & efficiency
nodes.plain_meaning: Where your farm's energy comes from and how efficiently you use fuel and power.
nodes.why_it_matters: Renewable and efficient energy cuts both emissions and one of a farm's biggest bills.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Renewable energy on-farm, and fuel/electricity use per unit of output.
nodes.data_source: Utility bills + equipment records (receipts / self-report).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Utility statements and visible solar/wind installations back the claim.

nodes.label: Pasture Access & Living Conditions
nodes.plain_meaning: Animals actually living outdoors on pasture with room to move, not confined in feedlots or cages.
nodes.why_it_matters: Pasture-based systems are required under ROC (no CAFOs) and are what make grazing regenerative.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Time on pasture and space per animal, and the absence of confinement (CAFO).
nodes.data_source: Site audit + photos (third-party cert / photo evidence).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Confinement infrastructure and herd condition are obvious on an unannounced visit.

nodes.label: Time on pasture / outdoor access
nodes.plain_meaning: How much of the year your animals are actually out on living pasture.
nodes.why_it_matters: Genuine pasture time is the difference between regenerative grazing and industrial confinement.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Days/year and hours/day of real pasture / outdoor access.
nodes.data_source: Site audit + grazing records (third-party cert / self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Pasture wear, dung distribution and animal condition corroborate the claimed time outdoors.

nodes.label: Stocking density / space
nodes.plain_meaning: Giving each animal enough room, so pastures and barns are not overcrowded.
nodes.why_it_matters: Appropriate space is a welfare basic and prevents the pasture damage of overstocking.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Animals per acre / per area versus recognized welfare standards.
nodes.data_source: Herd count + measured area (site audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: A head-count against measured area on a site visit is straightforward to verify.

nodes.label: Animal Health & Low-stress Handling
nodes.plain_meaning: Keeping animals healthy through good conditions and gentle handling, without routine painful alterations or heavy antibiotics.
nodes.why_it_matters: ROC bans routine mutilations (beak-trimming, tail-docking, dehorning, mulesing) and rewards preventive health over drugs.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Absence of routine physical alterations, low-stress handling, and a preventive-health / low-antibiotic record.
nodes.data_source: Veterinary records + site audit (third-party cert / vet records).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Physical alterations and drug use are visible on animals and in vet/treatment records.

nodes.label: No routine physical alterations
nodes.plain_meaning: Not doing painful routine procedures like beak-trimming, tail-docking, dehorning or mulesing.
nodes.why_it_matters: Avoiding these is a core ROC animal-welfare requirement and a clear welfare marker.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Herd/flock free of prohibited alterations, with pain relief where a procedure is unavoidable.
nodes.data_source: Site audit / visual inspection (third-party cert).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Alterations are physically evident on the animals themselves.

nodes.label: Preventive health & low antibiotic use
nodes.plain_meaning: Keeping animals well through good living conditions, so you rarely need antibiotics — and never as routine growth promoters.
nodes.why_it_matters: Preventive health signals genuine welfare and avoids the antibiotic overuse that drives resistance.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Antibiotic/medication use per animal, and no routine or prophylactic dosing.
nodes.data_source: Veterinary & treatment records (vet records / third-party cert).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Treatment logs and vet records document real drug use; residues are testable.

nodes.label: Fair Labor & Worker Wellbeing
nodes.plain_meaning: Paying a fair, living wage, keeping work safe, and never using forced or child labor.
nodes.why_it_matters: Social fairness is a required ROC pillar — regenerative cannot rest on exploited people.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Wage levels versus a living-wage benchmark, the safety record, and no forced/child labor.
nodes.data_source: Payroll records + worker interviews (third-party cert / audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Payroll audits and confidential worker interviews are far harder to fake than a self-declaration.

nodes.label: Living wage & safe conditions
nodes.plain_meaning: Workers earn at least a local living wage and work in safe, dignified conditions.
nodes.why_it_matters: Fair pay and safety are the baseline of a just farm and a required certification criterion.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Wages versus a recognized living-wage benchmark, plus documented safety practices.
nodes.data_source: Payroll records + site safety audit (third-party cert).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Payroll records and worker interviews cross-check the stated wages.

nodes.label: No forced or child labor
nodes.plain_meaning: No forced, bonded or child labor anywhere in the operation, including seasonal crews.
nodes.why_it_matters: This is a non-negotiable floor for any credible fairness claim.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Documented labor practices, freedom of association, and age verification.
nodes.data_source: Audit + worker interviews (third-party cert).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Independent worker interviews and documentation audits detect coercion that a form cannot.

nodes.label: Local Economy & Access
nodes.plain_meaning: Selling into the local foodshed and making sure real people in the community can actually afford and get the food.
nodes.why_it_matters: Short local supply chains keep money and food in the community — the point of a decentralized food network.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Food miles / local sales share, plus affordability and community-access programs.
nodes.data_source: Sales-geography records + program documentation (platform data / self-report).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Delivery geography and program participation are documented in platform and sales records.

nodes.label: Local sourcing / food miles
nodes.plain_meaning: How close your eaters are to your farm — how far the food travels to reach them.
nodes.why_it_matters: Shorter distances mean fresher food, lower emissions and a stronger local economy.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Share of sales within a defined local radius, and average delivery distance.
nodes.data_source: Sales-geography / platform delivery data.
nodes.gaming_resistance: Delivery addresses and platform logistics data make distances directly measurable.

nodes.label: Affordability & community access
nodes.plain_meaning: Making your food reachable for ordinary local people — through fair pricing, SNAP/coupon acceptance, gleaning or donation.
nodes.why_it_matters: Regenerative food that only the wealthy can buy fails the community half of the mission.
nodes.evaluable_proxy: Participation in access programs, and the share of product reaching lower-income channels.
nodes.data_source: Program records + sales data (self-report + spot-audit).
nodes.gaming_resistance: Program enrollment and transaction records document real access, not intentions.

edges.note: direct sales feed the local economy
